CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions are stated clearly early, with methods and evaluations (TRANSFER, WAC, SIM-WAP) described in a sensible order and experiments tied to specific claims about complementary strengths. Notation and writing are reported as good and related work coverage is thorough, and figures/tables/examples appear effective for illustrating qualitative differences between models. Presentation is weakened by missing hyperparameter details and no code release, which harms reproducibility and makes some methodological choices (e.g. ridge regression, label weighting) harder to scrutinize; the reduced-vocabulary setup is stated but its implications could be more explicitly motivated. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear intro→methods→experiments→conclusion flow and the main contributions are stated early (combining TRANSFER, WAC, SIM-WAP and showing complementary gains). Notation and methods are presented clearly and writing quality is reported as good, with effective figures/tables and thorough related work coverage. Minor presentation gaps remain (no hyperparameter reporting, code availability not mentioned, and the reduced 159-word vocabulary is noted but could use clearer discussion of generalisation limits), so I judge it as well-written with only minor presentation issues.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized and the main contributions are stated clearly early, with methods and evaluations (TRANSFER, WAC, SIM-WAP) described in a sensible order and experiments tied to specific claims about complementary strengths. Notation and writing are reported as good and related work coverage is thorough, and figures/tables/examples appear effective for illustrating qualitative differences between models. Presentation is weakened by missing hyperparameter details and no code release, which harms reproducibility and makes some methodological choices (e.g. ridge regression, label weighting) harder to scrutinize; the reduced-vocabulary setup is stated but its implications could be more explicitly motivated.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear intro→methods→experiments→conclusion flow and the main contributions are stated early (combining TRANSFER, WAC, SIM-WAP and showing complementary gains). Notation and methods are presented clearly and writing quality is reported as good, with effective figures/tables and thorough related work coverage. Minor presentation gaps remain (no hyperparameter reporting, code availability not mentioned, and the reduced 159-word vocabulary is noted but could use clearer discussion of generalisation limits), so I judge it as well-written with only minor presentation issues.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper evaluates its proposals against reasonable, directly relevant baselines (WAC, TRANSFER, SIM-WAP) under the same datasets and metrics (hit@k, embedding cosine), and explicitly discusses when the prior methods succeed or fail relative to the combination. The authors analyze qualitative trade-offs (semantic plausibility vs. referential precision) and situate their work with respect to related literature (e.g., Ordonez et al.), but the setup uses a reduced 159-word vocabulary and lacks reporting of hyperparameter/variance details—limitations that constitute a minor omission rather than a fatal comparison gap. | B(4): The paper evaluates its three models (TRANSFER, WAC, SIM-WAP) against each other using the same datasets (REFERIT/SAIAPR), matching metrics (hit@k, embedding cosine) and conditions, and provides both quantitative and qualitative analyses showing complementary error patterns. It acknowledges limitations and discusses when prior approaches can match or outperform components (e.g., TRANSFER’s semantic but coarse predictions) and notes scale differences relative to work like Ordonez et al. While related work coverage is thorough, the evaluation uses a reduced 159-word vocabulary and does not appear to include some larger-scale or more recent baselines, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates its proposals against reasonable, directly relevant baselines (WAC, TRANSFER, SIM-WAP) under the same datasets and metrics (hit@k, embedding cosine), and explicitly discusses when the prior methods succeed or fail relative to the combination. The authors analyze qualitative trade-offs (semantic plausibility vs. referential precision) and situate their work with respect to related literature (e.g., Ordonez et al.), but the setup uses a reduced 159-word vocabulary and lacks reporting of hyperparameter/variance details—limitations that constitute a minor omission rather than a fatal comparison gap.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates its three models (TRANSFER, WAC, SIM-WAP) against each other using the same datasets (REFERIT/SAIAPR), matching metrics (hit@k, embedding cosine) and conditions, and provides both quantitative and qualitative analyses showing complementary error patterns. It acknowledges limitations and discusses when prior approaches can match or outperform components (e.g., TRANSFER’s semantic but coarse predictions) and notes scale differences relative to work like Ordonez et al. While related work coverage is thorough, the evaluation uses a reduced 159-word vocabulary and does not appear to include some larger-scale or more recent baselines, so comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical evaluation is solid—comparing complementary models across datasets with meaningful metrics and a thoughtful discussion of limitations—yet both note missing reproducibility details (hyperparameters, variance across runs, code) and limited sensitivity/ablation analyses. Given the substantive experimental contribution but the methodological gaps that prevent full replicability and stronger causal claims, a score slightly above middle is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides substantive empirical comparisons across three complementary modeling approaches (TRANSFER, WAC, SIM-WAP) on multiple datasets with appropriate metrics and a nuanced discussion of limitations, so experiments are adequate in scope. However, from the summary I see no reported hyperparameters, variance across runs, statistical significance, or controlled ablations that isolate each component (e.g. why ridge regression, why score aggregation), which weakens the causal claims. Related work is thorough and failure modes are discussed, but reproducibility details and sensitivity analyses are insufficient to fully support the main theoretical claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are strong in comparing complementary models (TRANSFER, WAC, SIM-WAP) across multiple datasets and meaningful metrics (hit@k, embedding cosine) and demonstrate aggregation gains and qualitative complementary behaviors, which I value. However, key reproducibility details are missing (no hyperparameters or code mentioned) and there is limited sensitivity/ablation analysis of aggregation choices and model hyperparameters; the vocabulary reduction is also a notable constraint. Related work is treated thoroughly and limitations are discussed, so overall the empirical evaluation is solid but with minor gaps preventing it from being fully comprehensive.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper offers a coherent, empirically useful combination of existing techniques and that SIM-WAP provides a meaningful, pragmatic twist by using continuous similarity labels. However, the core methods are largely extensions and integrations of known components rather than a new theoretical framework, making the contribution an incremental applied advance rather than a fundamentally novel one. The paper’s careful analysis and demonstrated complementary gains justify credit above minimal originality, but not the highest novelty rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a coherent combination of existing components (ridge-regression cross-modal mapping, per-word visual classifiers) with a moderately novel twist in SIM-WAP (training per-word predictors on continuous distributional similarity labels) and an aggregation strategy that empirically improves naming. However, the core methods are largely reapplications of known techniques (word2vec, GoogleNet features, WAC, TRANSFER) and the novelty resides mainly in model integration and an empirical training signal rather than a new formal framework or theoretical insight. The authors clearly differentiate their contributions and document complementary failure modes, but from a theory-first perspective the work is an incremental, applied advance rather than a fundamentally new idea.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable, if pragmatic, novelty by proposing SIM-WAP (per-word predictors trained with continuous similarity labels) and empirically demonstrating that aggregating cross-modal mapping (TRANSFER), visual classifiers (WAC) and SIM-WAP yields complementary gains for object naming. The framing—explicitly combining distributional and referential signals and analyzing where each model excels (e.g. fine-grained referential distinctions vs. semantic appropriateness, singular/plural zero-shot cases)—clearly differentiates contributions from prior work and supplies actionable insights. The techniques are a non-trivial extension of existing mappings/classifier approaches rather than merely reusing off-the-shelf methods, so the work would inform experts interested in cross-modal grounding and zero-shot naming.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical claims are generally supported by well-designed experiments and sensible baselines, but that the paper omits important reproducibility and robustness details (hyperparameters, variance/statistical tests) and lacks some controlled ablations and theoretical justification. These limitations reduce the work's rigor but do not indicate outright technical flaws or incorrectness in the core methodology and results. Given the solid empirical support tempered by missing methodological details, a score of 4 best reflects the paper's overall soundness.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and presents experiments across REFERIT/SAIAPR with three complementary models (TRANSFER, WAC, SIM-WAP) and combination gains, but it lacks theoretical justification for key design choices (e.g. why ridge regression and similarity-weighted labels) and does not report hyperparameters, variance across runs, or statistical significance. The methodology includes sensible baselines, yet I see missing controlled ablations isolating each component's contribution and limited discussion of failure modes given the known mismatch between distributional and referential spaces. Overall the results are promising but some claims about generalization (especially zero-shot and SIM-WAP advantages) are insufficiently supported by rigorous analysis and statistical validation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and primarily supported by empirical evidence: head-to-head comparisons of TRANSFER, WAC and SIM-WAP on hit@k and embedding-distance metrics demonstrate complementary behavior and combined gains, and the analysis of error types (semantic vs referential) aligns with the claims about each method. The methodology is broadly sound with appropriate baselines and sensible metrics, but the summary indicates missing experimental details (no hyperparameter reporting, code not mentioned, no mention of statistical significance tests) and a restricted vocabulary that limits generality. There are no obvious mathematical flaws, but reliance on distributional similarity for referential tasks is acknowledged as a limitation and could cause unsupported generalization beyond the tested setup. Overall the technical work is solid with only minor reproducibility and scope concerns that do not overturn the main conclusions.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important, practical problem and demonstrates clear empirical gains by combining complementary signals for object naming, likely influencing work on grounded naming and multimodal lexical induction. Concerns about limited theoretical justification, reduced vocabulary size, and missing experimental details and variance reporting temper claims of broad impact; nevertheless, the demonstrated complementary strengths and empirical improvements justify a positive moderate-to-strong impact score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—combining distributional and referential signals for object naming—and shows empirically that three complementary mechanisms (TRANSFER, WAC, SIM-WAP) yield gains when aggregated, which is useful to the vision+language community. However, from a theory-first perspective the work offers limited formal justification for its model combinations (e.g. why ridge regression or similarity-weighted real-valued labels should be expected to improve zero-shot generalization) and reports no hyperparameter details or statistical variance, weakening confidence that the empirical gains will generalize. The reduced 159-word vocabulary, admitted alignment issues between distributional and referential meaning, and lack of ablation/statistical rigor constrain the paper’s broader impact and uptake. Still, demonstrating complementary strengths of mapping vs. classifier vs. similarity-weighted predictors does open modest follow-on directions for principled probabilistic modelling of cross-modal lexical meaning.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important, practical problem—object naming and zero-shot referring—in a way that yields clear empirical gains by combining complementary signals (TRANSFER cross-modal mapping, WAC visual classifiers, and SIM-WAP similarity-weighted predictors) and reporting hit@k and embedding-distance improvements on REFERIT/SAIAPR. The focus on complementary error modes (semantic plausibility vs. referential precision) and demonstrated improvements, including better handling of singular/plural zero-shot cases, make the approach likely to influence labs working on grounded naming and multimodal lexical induction. Limitations such as a reduced 159-word vocabulary and potential misalignment between distributional and referential meaning temper transformational claims but the work opens concrete directions for ensemble-style grounding and improved generalisation.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is a strong fit for the vision+language community, uses appropriate datasets, baselines and metrics, and makes a useful empirical contribution showing complementary gains from combining distributional and referential signals. I rate it a 4 because, while the evaluation and positioning are appropriate for a top venue, methodological reporting (hyperparameters, code availability) and lack of variance/significance and finer-grained ablation limit its completeness and prevent a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper fits squarely within top-tier vision+NLP venues: it tackles cross-modal object naming using distributional and referential signals and evaluates on REFERIT/SAIAPR with standard metrics (hit@k, embedding cosine), which is relevant to the community. However, from a theory-first reviewer perspective there are methodological shortcomings that temper a perfect fit: hyperparameters are not reported, code isn’t mentioned, and the paper appears to lack statistical variance/ significance reporting and rigorous ablation isolating each component’s contribution. Despite these issues the problem formulation, baselines (WAC, TRANSFER, SIM-WAP), and evaluation protocol are appropriate for a top NLP/ML conference, so I rate it a good (but not perfect) fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper squarely targets the vision+language community and uses methods, datasets and metrics (REFERIT/SAIAPR, ImageNet features, hit@k, embedding cosine) that are standard at top NLP/ML venues. The work is empirically driven, compares sensible baselines (TRANSFER, WAC, SIM-WAP), demonstrates complementary gains from aggregating distributional and referential signals, and addresses practical issues like zero-shot naming and singular/plural distinctions—topics of clear interest to this audience. Experimental setup and evaluation align with the expectations of the venue, and the positioning and related work coverage are appropriate for researchers in multimodal semantics and grounded language tasks.

main_claims: Combining distributional (word2vec) and referential (visual) information by aggregating predictions from cross-modal mapping (TRANSFER), visual classifiers (WAC), and similarity-weighted predictors (SIM-WAP) improves object naming accuracy over individual models.

Direct cross-modal mapping (TRANSFER) tends to produce semantically appropriate and mutually similar candidate names but can fail to make fine-grained referential distinctions, while visual word classifiers (WAC) capture subtle visual distinctions but may produce taxonomically distant errors.

SIM-WAP, which trains per-word predictors using continuous similarity labels from a distributional space, generalizes better in zero-shot naming (especially singular/plural cases) than TRANSFER or WAC.
The three approaches capture complementary aspects of referential word meaning, and their combination yields clear overall improvement in standard naming tasks.
_summary.methodology: They extract 1031-d visual region features using GoogleNet (plus 7 location features) and use 400-d word2vec embeddings; they implement three models—TRANSFER: Ridge regression mapping visual vectors to word2vec and nearest-neighbor decoding; WAC: per-word visual classifiers (binary); SIM-WAP: per-word predictors trained with real-valued_similarity labels from word2vec—and evaluate individual and combined predictions (score aggregation over top-10).
metrics: cosine distance between word2vec embeddings of predicted vs. gold nouns
limitations: Distributional lexical similarity does not always align with referential meaning, which can mislead models that rely on distributional space.
TRANSFER (cross-modal mapping) can fail to make desired fine-grained distinctions despite producing semantically reasonable candidates.
The experiments use a reduced vocabulary of 159 entry-level names (smaller scale than some prior work such as Ordonez et al., 2016).
Zero-shot naming remains challenging and performance depends on lexical relation (e.g., hypernym vs. singular/plural) between unknown and known names.
_summary.topic_area: Vision+NLP - referring expression generation / object naming (cross-modal semantics)

reviewer_a.name: Theory-First Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in computational semantics with a track record of formal models of meaning and a history of publishing in top NLP/ML venues.
traits: Penalises papers that lack formal/theoretical analysis or clear probabilistic justification for proposed model combinations (will deduct points if only empirical heuristics are offered).
Requires thorough ablation and error analysis: downgrades papers that do not isolate contributions of each component with controlled experiments.
Demands reporting of statistical significance and variance across runs; penalises papers that present only single-run aggregate numbers without confidence intervals.
Penalises weak justification for design choices (e.g., why ridge regression, why similarity-weighted labels) and missing discussion of failure modes.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Critical, focused on theoretical grounding and rigorous empirical protocol; willing to accept empirical results only if accompanied by clear explanatory analysis and statistical rigor.

reviewer_b.name: Empirical Multimodal Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Applied machine learning researcher specializing in vision+language systems with several empirical papers on cross-modal grounding and zero-shot learning.
traits: Rewards papers that introduce practical, well-motivated methods combining complementary information sources (will give credit for demonstrating complementary gains).
Favors empirical novelty and strong benchmark improvements, especially when methods improve zero-shot/generalisation and handle real-world issues like singular/plural distinctions.
Values sound engineering and reproducibility: grants credit if authors evaluate across multiple baselines, report meaningful metrics (e.g., top-k), and provide implementation details/data splits.
Prefers clear demonstrations of complementary model behavior (e.g., qualitative examples showing different error types) and pragmatic ensemble/aggregation strategies.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and results-oriented: emphasizes empirical evidence of usefulness and generalisation of methods, and rewards clear gains and well-executed experimental comparisons.
